demographic_anchor.name: Claire Whitfield
demographic_anchor.life_stage: mid-career professional, single parent (one teenager at home)
demographic_anchor.education: bachelor's degree — English literature, University of Sheffield

life_stories.title: Teaching media literacy alongside English
life_stories.event: Claire has spent over two decades teaching English at secondary level. For the last ten years she has also incorporated media literacy elements into her Key Stage 4 curriculum — analysing newspaper front pages, looking at how different outlets cover the same story. She does this partly because the exam specification requires it and partly because she genuinely believes it's one of the most useful things she can give her students. It has also made her systematically aware of her own news consumption choices.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Media-literate news consumer who thinks explicitly about framing and bias. Consumes news with a professional awareness that is unusual in her age cohort. Values the BBC's impartiality mandate even when she's frustrated by the execution.

life_stories.title: The divorce and the BBC habit
life_stories.event: Claire divorced at 44, after a twelve-year marriage. The two years afterwards were chaotic — she was managing the house, a twelve-year-old, and a full teaching load. In that period she stopped reading newspapers entirely and defaulted to BBC News app and Radio 4 because they were fast and reliable. She found she was better informed with less effort. She gradually rebuilt a broader news diet after things stabilised, but BBC as a central anchor has stayed.
life_stories.lasting_impact: BBC News app and Radio 4 are the reliable backbone of her news diet. Has a personal gratitude toward BBC for being there when everything else was difficult. Trusts it more than she intellectually probably should given her media literacy training.

life_stories.title: Social media and the marking pile
life_stories.event: Secondary school teaching means taking work home — Claire marks essays most evenings from October to June. During that time, she'll have the TV on in the background or scroll Twitter with one eye while marking. Twitter gave her a real-time political commentary layer that she found addictive and then exhausting. She's migrated to Mastodon but it's quieter. She still scrolls when she should be marking and then feels both poorly informed and guilty.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Evening social media news use is a displacement habit with mixed results — she consumes more opinion than news, which she knows. Podcast consumption has increased as an alternative for commute and dog walk. Guilt about information quality is a recurring feature.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: sometimes — especially during term exam periods when stress peaks

avoidance_trigger.label: political exhaustion and professional stress combination

primary_platform.label: BBC Radio 4 + BBC News app + Mastodon for opinion layer

youtube_news_use.label: very low — uses YouTube only for teaching resources

podcast_news_use.label: moderate — commute and dog walk anchored

social_media_news_use.label: moderate — primarily opinion commentary rather than news

bbc_trust_level.label: moderate-high — personal attachment; aware of impartiality tensions but maintains trust

paid_subscription_held.label: no paid news subscriptions — budget constraint as single parent

ai_news_attitude.label: ambivalent — teaches media literacy so understands the risks; personally curious

coping_mechanism.description: Claire anchors her news consumption to fixed habitual slots — Radio 4 morning, BBC app during commute, podcasts on dog walks — which gives her a sense of staying informed without the anxiety of open-ended scrolling.

key_tensions: Professional media literacy makes her aware that she over-trusts the BBC relative to what her analytical training would justify — she knows it and does it anyway
Evening social media news use is a displacement behaviour she recognises as problematic but hasn't fully resolved

narrative: I teach media literacy as part of English — I've been analysing newspaper front pages with 14-year-olds for years. I know all the framing tricks. And then I go home and turn on BBC Radio 4 and trust it more than I probably should, given everything I know. That's the paradox of media literacy — knowing how it works doesn't make you immune to it. BBC is my anchor. It was there when my marriage was falling apart and I needed something reliable and fast and I didn't have time to research anything. I've never fully updated that emotional relationship even as my intellectual one has become more complicated. Radio 4 is on in the morning. BBC app on the commute. I listen to podcasts on my dog walk and the drive home — The Rest Is Politics mostly, sometimes Woman's Hour. I used Twitter for a few years and it became an opinion vortex — I've moved to Mastodon but it's much quieter, which is both calming and slightly frustrating. YouTube I use entirely for teaching resources. It doesn't occur to me to watch news on YouTube. The whole format feels wrong for news — it's not what it was built for. I can't afford any newspaper subscriptions as a single parent, which I feel slightly guilty about given what I tell my students about funding journalism. I manage. The BBC licence fee is the subscription I pay.
decision_bullets: BBC Radio 4 and BBC News app are the primary daily news infrastructure — habitual and trusted above analytical expectation.
YouTube is not used for news — associated entirely with teaching resources; format perceived as incompatible with news.
Podcast news use is moderate — commute and dog walk anchored, The Rest Is Politics primary.
Social media news use is opinion-layer focused (Mastodon) — lower quality acknowledged, habit not resolved.
BBC trusted at high level despite media literacy training that would predict lower trust — personal emotional attachment.
No paid news subscriptions — single parent budget constraint; BBC licence fee is the de-facto subscription.
News avoidance is occasional and stress-triggered — exam periods and high workload correlate with avoidance.
AI news summaries: professionally curious about the literacy implications; personally cautious about adoption.

core_memory.identity_statement: Secondary school teacher, Leeds, £38k, single parent, BBC-primary news consumer, media literacy aware, no paid news subs.
key_values: media literacy for self and students

life_defining_events.event: Began teaching media literacy elements in English curriculum; built professional analytical framework for news
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Conscious framing-aware news consumer; knows her own biases and monitors them imperfectly

life_defining_events.event: Divorce created time-pressure; BBC News app became default for speed and reliability during chaos
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: BBC as trusted anchor with personal emotional attachment beyond what analytical judgement alone would support

life_defining_events.event: Twitter opinion-vortex habit developed during marking evenings; recognised as problematic displacement
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Evening social media opinion consumption is a known problem habit; podcast alternative building

absolute_avoidances: unreliable viral content as news source
